1.synthesis:
Two years after the night visions (Kislev of Darius’s fourth year, December 518 BCE), a delegation from Bethel sends men to Jerusalem to ask the priests and prophets a question: “Should I mourn and fast in the fifth month as I have done these many years?” The fifth-month fast (tsom hahamishi) commemorated the burning of Jerusalem and temple by Babylon (2 Kings 25:8–9); now that the exiles have returned and the temple is nearly rebuilt, the ritual rationale seems to have expired. The question is sincere, practical, and community-shaping: does a practice instituted in grief over catastrophe continue when the catastrophe is being reversed?

The question opens a four-chapter discourse (chs.7–8) that ranks among the most theologically rich sections of Zechariah. YHWH’s response to the fasting question does not give a yes-or-no answer immediately; it first reframes the entire question by exposing the motives behind the practice, then moves through historical recollection to eschatological promise. The journey from “should we fast?” to “the fasts will become feasts” covers the full sweep of covenant history and ends in the ingathering of the nations.
1.historical_context: Four fasts commemorated the destruction of Jerusalem: fourth month (fall of the city), fifth month (burning of temple), seventh month (assassination of Gedaliah), tenth month (beginning of the siege). The question about the fifth month is thus the central fast of the four, commemorating the temple’s destruction in the year that the second temple was nearing completion. The Bethel delegation reflects a diaspora community still organized around a grief-practice that may have outlived its function — or perhaps asking whether such practices should outlive their historical occasion.
1.literary_structure: The three-verse prologue (date, delegation, question) frames chs.7–8 as a single extended response. YHWH’s answer develops in stages: challenge of motive (7:4–7), historical recollection of prophetic failure (7:8–14), promises of restoration (8:1–17), and climactic transformation of fasts to feasts (8:18–23). The question asked in 7:3 is not answered until 8:19 — the delay is itself pedagogical.
1.theological_application: Communities that practice grief-rituals should periodically ask whether the ritual is serving its formative purpose or has become rote performance. The question from Bethel is not cynical; it is theologically engaged — they want to know whether YHWH wants them to continue, not whether they can stop. The willingness to ask a communal practice’s purpose before YHWH models the kind of reflective worship that YHWH’s subsequent response will validate.
1.christology: The fasting question anticipates Jesus’ teaching on fasting in Matthew 9:14–15: “Can the wedding guests mourn while the bridegroom is with them? The days will come when the bridegroom is taken from them, and then they will fast.” The presence of the Bridegroom transforms the logic of fasting — exactly the transformation Zechariah 8:19 will announce. The answer to “should we fast?” requires knowing who is present.

4.synthesis:
YHWH’s first response challenges the entire framework of the question: “When you fasted and mourned in the fifth month and in the seventh, for these seventy years, was it for me that you fasted?” (Zechariah 7:5). The question cuts to the root: fasting for whom? And eating and drinking for whom? The implication is that both the grief-rituals and the regular meals have been performed in self-referential modes — for the community’s own benefit, grief, or gratification — not as expressions of covenant orientation toward YHWH. YHWH then appeals to the earlier prophets: were they not saying the same thing? The words of the former prophets are not obsolete; the question the Bethel delegation brings is answered by listening to what YHWH had been saying all along through Jeremiah, Amos, Isaiah, Micah.

The rhetorical force is devastating: seventy years of fasting that was performed “for yourselves” produced no spiritual fruit precisely because it was misdirected. Ritual without covenantal orientation is empty, however painful the emotion it enacts. YHWH’s concern is not whether fasting happens but whether the heart that fasts is turned toward him rather than toward its own grief-performance.
4.historical_context: The appeal to “when Jerusalem was inhabited and prosperous” (v.7) refers to the pre-exilic era when the prophets were speaking — Amos, Micah, Jeremiah — and the land was still secure. This is historically honest: the prophetic message of justice over ritual was not delivered only in crisis but was the consistent covenant call throughout the monarchy period. The pairing of fasting (fifth month) and celebration (eating and drinking at feasts) as parallel questions in v.6 is rhetorically sharp: both can be performed for self, not for YHWH.
4.literary_structure: The double question structure (vv.5–6: “was it for me?” for both fasting and feasting) creates a rhetorical chiasm: the problem is not the form of the practice but the orientation of the heart behind it. V.7 then deploys the authority of the earlier prophets as corroboration, showing that YHWH has been asking this question throughout Israel’s history. The historical reference sets up the fuller recollection of prophetic failure in vv.8–14.
4.theological_application: The “for whom?” question applies to all communal religious practice: worship, prayer, fasting, celebration. Practices performed for communal self-image, emotional catharsis, cultural continuity, or social bonding are all forms of practicing “for yourselves.” The reorientation YHWH requires is not a different set of practices but a different direction of the will: from self-referential performance to genuine covenant engagement with YHWH. Isaiah 58’s “Is not this the fast I choose?” says the same thing in a different register.
4.christology: Jesus’ teaching on fasting in Matthew 6:16–18 (“when you fast, do not look gloomy as the hypocrites do, for they disfigure their faces that their fasting may be seen by others”) directly applies Zechariah’s “for whom?” challenge. The fast that is performed for an audience of one — the Father who sees in secret — is the fast YHWH was asking for through Zechariah. The orientation of heart that YHWH demands is supplied by Christ who did all things “for the glory of God” (1 Corinthians 10:31).

8.synthesis:
YHWH now articulates what the earlier prophets were actually saying: “Render true judgments, show kindness and mercy to one another, do not oppress the widow, the fatherless, the sojourner, or the poor, and let none of you devise evil against another in your heart.” (Zechariah 7:9–10). The content of the prophetic word is justice and mercy in community life, precisely the covenantal obligations the Torah had always stipulated. But the fathers refused to hear. They “stopped their ears” (sichlelu et uznam), made their hearts as adamant (shamir, the hardest stone, used for cutting), lest they hear the Torah and the words YHWH sent by his Spirit through the earlier prophets. The result: “Therefore great wrath came from YHWH of armies. And as I called and they did not hear, so they called and I did not hear, says YHWH of armies.” (Zechariah 7:12–13). The symmetry is exact: the people closed their ears to YHWH’s voice; YHWH closed his ears to their crisis-cry. Then the whirlwind of exile: “I scattered them with a whirlwind among all the nations,” and the pleasant land became desolate behind them.

The historical recollection provides the essential context for evaluating the current fasting question. The fasts commemorated a catastrophe that resulted from the fathers’ refusal of exactly what YHWH is now restating. The right response to remembering that catastrophe is not perpetual mourning but the justice and mercy the fathers refused. Zechariah thus refocuses grief from backward-looking ritual to forward-oriented practice.
8.historical_context: The summary of prophetic content (vv.9–10) draws on the classic prophetic tradition: Amos 5:24 (justice), Micah 6:8 (justice/kindness/humility), Jeremiah 7:5–6 (widow/orphan/foreigner), Isaiah 1:17 (same). The hardening of heart as diamond/flint (shamir) is distinctive: this is not gradual spiritual dullness but active, willed resistance to heard prophetic word. The reversal “they called and I did not hear” (v.13) echoes Proverbs 1:24–28 (Wisdom calling and being refused, then refusing the caller in distress) and anticipates Isaiah 65:12.
8.literary_structure: The passage functions as a historical prosecution (covenant lawsuit) building from prophetic demand (vv.9–10) through people’s refusal (vv.11–12) to divine symmetrical response (v.13) to consequence (v.14). The structural symmetry of “they would not listen” / “I would not listen” is the lex talionis of covenant relationship applied to the hearing of prayer. The whirlwind and desolate land (v.14) close the historical review and frame the present restoration as grace beyond what the sequence deserved.
8.theological_application: The adamant heart (“hearts like flint”) as the mode of prophetic resistance shows that spiritual deafness is not intellectual failure but moral choice: the fathers heard and refused. This is the condition against which Ezekiel’s promise of a “heart of flesh” replacing the “heart of stone” is directed (Ezekiel 36:26). Communities that ask ritual questions (“should we fast?”) while ignoring justice questions (“are we caring for widows, orphans, and sojourners?”) are rehearsing the fathers’ mistake. The prophetic insistence that covenant faithfulness is measured in treatment of the vulnerable never changes.
8.christology: The Spirit speaking through the earlier prophets (v.12) anticipates the Spirit’s fullness in Christ. Jesus summarizes the prophetic content of vv.9–10 in Matthew 23:23 (“the weightier matters of the law: justice, mercy, faithfulness”). The divine non-hearing of those who refused to hear (v.13) becomes the “I never knew you” of Matthew 7:23. Christ’s hardening of heart diagnosis in Mark 8:17 draws on the same covenant category Zechariah employs, and his death breaks the cycle by opening what the adamant heart had sealed.
